coredata.dc:title: Enhancing photovoltaic performance of graphene quantum dot/Ru(II) nanocomposites through strategic ligand modifications

coredata.dc:description: The effectiveness of Ruthenium(II) polypyridyl complexes as photosensitizers for dye-sensitized solar cells is well established. These complexes exhibit tunable excited-state properties, but their absorption is typically limited to the visible spectrum. To overcome this limitation, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been introduced as a valuable enhancement to boost the light-harvesting capabilities of these complexes. This research investigates the optoelectronic properties and energy conversion efficiency of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites featuring various substituents—4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl, phenyl, 4-BODIPY-phenyl, and pyren-1-yl—attached to the terpyridine ligand of the Ru(II) complex. The strategic placement of these substituents significantly affects the electronic structure, charge transfer dynamics, and photovoltaic performance of the nanocomposites. Our findings show that incorporating a pyrenyl substituent enhances spatial charge separation and interfragment charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, resulting in the highest energy conversion efficiency of 19.3 % among the nanocomposites studied. In contrast, the oligoether/phenyl and BODIPY substituents yield lower efficiencies of 14.6 % and 18.2 %, respectively. GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, particularly those with the pyrenyl substituent, demonstrate improved photoinduced electron injection into semiconductor substrates, leading to enhanced photovoltaic performance. This study highlights the importance of strategically modifying ligands to optimize the optoelectronic properties and charge dynamics in GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, ultimately maximizing their efficiency in solar energy conversion.

The highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of Ru(II) complexes alongside GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites featuring different substituents at the tpy ligand. The blue and red dashed lines signify the conduction band minmum (CBM; -4.0 eV) of TiO2 and the HOMO (-4.8 eV) of the I−/I3- redox pair. The Eg denotes the band gaps of isolated Ru(II) complexes and GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites.
Projected density of states (PDOS) of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites with various substituents. The red, blue and black line denote GQD, Ru(II) complex and GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposite respectively.
Schematic illustration of charge separation dynamics in (a) GQD‒Ru(II)1 and (b) GQD‒Ru(II)2 and GQD‒Ru(II)3.

Absorption spectra of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites with various substituents. The black dashed line denotes the maximum absorption wavelength. The S1 and Smax denote the first absorption peak and absorption maxima, respectively.
HOMO and LUMO levels and chemical reactivity parameters

head.ce:title: Enhancing photovoltaic performance of graphene quantum dot/Ru(II) nanocomposites through strategic ligand modifications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou, Fujian 362000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effectiveness of Ruthenium(II) polypyridyl complexes as photosensitizers for dye-sensitized solar cells is well established. These complexes exhibit tunable excited-state properties, but their absorption is typically limited to the visible spectrum. To overcome this limitation, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been introduced as a valuable enhancement to boost the light-harvesting capabilities of these complexes. This research investigates the optoelectronic properties and energy conversion efficiency of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites featuring various substituents—4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl, phenyl, 4-BODIPY-phenyl, and pyren-1-yl—attached to the terpyridine ligand of the Ru(II) complex. The strategic placement of these substituents significantly affects the electronic structure, charge transfer dynamics, and photovoltaic performance of the nanocomposites. Our findings show that incorporating a pyrenyl substituent enhances spatial charge separation and interfragment charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, resulting in the highest energy conversion efficiency of 19.3 % among the nanocomposites studied. In contrast, the oligoether/phenyl and BODIPY substituents yield lower efficiencies of 14.6 % and 18.2 %, respectively. GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, particularly those with the pyrenyl substituent, demonstrate improved photoinduced electron injection into semiconductor substrates, leading to enhanced photovoltaic performance. This study highlights the importance of strategically modifying ligands to optimize the optoelectronic properties and charge dynamics in GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, ultimately maximizing their efficiency in solar energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing global energy demand and the challenges associated with fossil fuels have driven significant research into renewable energy technologies such as photovoltaics [1,2]. Solar cells convert sunlight directly into electricity, providing a clean energy alternative [3]. However, to increase the adoption of solar technology[4], power conversion efficiencies must be improved. This need has spurred investigations into novel light-harvesting materials capable of absorbing sunlight and generating photocurrents [5]. Extensive research has focused on transition metal complexes, especially Ru(II) polypyridyl complexes, as photosensitizers for dye-sensitized solar cells. These complexes are highly desirable due to their tunable excited-state properties, excellent visible light absorption, favorable redox characteristics, and long excited-state lifetimes, all of which contribute to efficient electron transfer for electricity generation [6–8]. By modifying the ligands, the optical and electrochemical properties of these complexes can be precisely adjusted, leading to enhanced light-harvesting capabilities and improved redox behavior [9]. Moreover, computational studies have demonstrated that the arrangement of frontier molecular orbitals in these complexes plays a crucial role in determining their power conversion efficiencies in dye-sensitized solar cells [10]. In conclusion, transition metal complexes, particularly Ru(II) polypyridyl complexes, hold great potential for optimizing solar energy conversion, making them highly promising candidates for photovoltaic applications.

Strategies to enhance the performance of Ru(II) complexes involve modifying ligands to extend π-conjugation [11,12] and incorporating electron-withdrawing groups [13,14]. For instance, Monika and colleagues use binuclear ruthenium(II) complexes as catalysts to facilitate the coupling of primary alcohols with 2-aminopyridine through dehydrogenation reactions, forming an imine intermediate. This approach offers a direct and efficient pathway to specific heterocycles under mild conditions, eliminating the need for oxidants or hydrogen acceptors [15]. Mahmood et al. studied ruthenium coordination compounds with modified polypyridine ligands in photocatalytic phosphorylation under visible light. By introducing electron-withdrawing substituents to the ligands, they were able to lower redox potentials, improve charge transfer, and significantly increase product yields compared to an unsubstituted reference complex [16]. Additionally, incorporating organic chromophores such as boron dipyrromethene (BODIPY) has broadened the absorption spectrum and generated long-lived excited states, enhancing photocurrents. This has been demonstrated in the works of Ziessel [17,18] and Hanan [19]. Although diimine ligands are commonly used in most reported Ru(II) complexes, bis-terpyridine Ru(II) complexes represent an understudied class in photovoltaics. The strong absorption of visible light and moderately long-lived excited states make these complexes promising light-harvesting molecules for solar energy applications [20–22]. The visible light absorption stems from metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) transitions, while the incorporation of π-conjugated chromophores such as BODIPY extends the excited state lifetimes [21–23]. These properties allow the complexes to absorb sunlight and transfer energized electrons, generating electricity. Research shows that metal-organic complexes modified with π-conjugated ligands can undergo photoinduced electron transfer when attached to TiO2 surfaces, producing photocurrents [24,25]. Further optimization of ligand structures and their attachment to semiconductor surfaces could enhance the performance of these complexes in dye-sensitized solar cells. Moreover, their chemical stability makes them suitable for robust solar devices. Exploring how ligand structures impact photophysical properties offers valuable insights into the design of bis-terpyridine Ru(II) complexes for photovoltaic applications.

While Ru(II) polypyridyl complexes have been widely studied in photovoltaics, their absorption is generally confined to the visible range. This limitation has prompted research into new light-harvesting materials capable of better utilizing the solar spectrum to generate electricity. One promising class of materials is graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional graphene nanomaterials typically less than 10 nm in size. GQDs exhibit unique optical and electronic properties due to quantum confinement and edge effects [26–29], making them highly effective in transferring energized electrons, and thus potential sensitizers in photovoltaic applications. Recent studies suggest that incorporating dye complexes onto graphene can facilitate electron transfer processes [24,25]. The combination of graphene with dye complexes can lead to panchromatic solar absorption and increased photocurrents. However, limited research has been done on the specific interactions between graphene and dye complexes. For example, Anju et al. demonstrated that graphene-dye hybrids can serve as highly sensitive optical sensors for various analytes, showing improved electron transfer and adsorption capacities compared to standalone materials [30]. Loryuenyong et al. found that the introduction of graphene oxide into TiO2 nanocomposites used as photoanodes in dye-sensitized solar cells enhanced their efficiency. With an optimal graphene content of 2.5 wt%, the power conversion efficiency increased by 47 %, reaching 9.81 %, surpassing that of pure TiO2 nanoparticles [31]. Similarly, Low et al. discovered that graphene/TiO2 nanocomposites improved light harvesting and charge collection, leading to higher power conversion efficiencies compared to pure TiO2. An optimal graphene content of 0.1 % resulted in a 36 % efficiency boost, increasing from 7.63 % to 10.4 % [32]. Renuka et al. presented a graphene-dye supramolecular assembly that demonstrated the potential for highly sensitive monitoring of fluoride ions at the parts-per-trillion level, showcasing its application in precise detection for environmental and biomedical purposes [33].

Density functional theory (DFT) simulations enable rapid, cost-efficient exploration of GQD/dye nanocomposite configurations and design parameters to optimize photovoltaic performance before experimental synthesis. By elucidating electronic structure and charge transfer dynamics, DFT provides predictive insights into material behavior, informing experiments for an iterative, mechanistic understanding to efficiently develop improved photovoltaic materials. Wazzan et al. computationally explored combining natural dye molecules (NDIX) from Ixora flowers with GQDs, finding the nanocomposites have improved light absorption and higher molar extinction coefficients compared to individual NDIX dyes [34]. Alsamani et al. studied catechin/GQD nanocomposites as hybrid photocatalysts for dye-sensitized solar cells, finding carboxyl-functionalized boron-doped GQDs tuned to optimize light absorption and charge separation, enhancing performance over individual components [35]. Zhao et al. studied GQDs combined with dyes to enhance solar cell photoelectric conversion efficiency [36]. Similarly, Wang et al. computationally found graphene sheets facilitate dye regeneration and charge transport in dye-sensitized solar cells, quantitatively demonstrating enhanced photocurrents, fill factors, and overall performance [37]. Cui et al. utilize computational methods to design and optimize GQD-based hybrid photocatalysts and photoelectric materials by tuning the transition metal complex components [24,25]. In summary, computations guide design of efficient, sustainable GQD/dye sensitizers.

To investigate the optoelectronic properties and energy conversion efficiency of a GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposite material composed of a GQD and a ruthenium (II) complex, we conducted calculations using a density functional theory (DFT) approach. Two series of derivatives with varying substituents were explored, including the 4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl group, phenyl group, 4-BODIPY-phenyl group, and pyren-1-yl group, all attached to the terpyridine (tpy) ligand of the Ru(II) complex. The 4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl group is expected to have minimal impact on the photophysical properties of the Ru(II) complex due to its inert nature. However, its electron-donating character might slightly red-shift both absorption and emission spectra. The introduction of the BODIPY group, with its extended π-conjugation, enhances the lifetime of triplet excited states associated with the Ru(II) complex by activating the low-energy 3π,π* state [38,39]. BODIPY's strong absorption in the visible light region [40,41] can also improve photo-harvesting in the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites. Similarly, the incorporation of a pyrene group extends the lifetime of triplet excited states originating from the Ru(II) complex due to the low-lying pyrene-based 3π,π* state [42,43]. However, the combined influence of both pyrene and BODIPY on the optical properties of the Ru(II) complex, as well as their effect on the performance of the GQD/Ru(II) hybrid system at the nanoscale, is not yet fully understood. A deeper understanding of these molecular-level phenomena could enable tunable photovoltaic performance in electronic devices based on GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, making it a functional feature in advanced solar energy applications.

The purpose of this research was to study how changes in the electronic structure of Ru(II) complexes are influenced by different substituents on the terpyridine (tpy) ligand. These substituents included 4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl, phenyl, 4-BODIPY-phenyl, and pyren-1-yl, as previously investigated experimentally [23]. For simplicity, the complexes were referred to as Ru(II)1, Ru(II)2, and Ru(II)3, as shown in Fig. 1 . In these structures, R1 represents the substituent on one tpy ligand. In Ru(II)1 and Ru(II)2, R1 is the 4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl group, which is expected to improve the solubility of the Ru(II) complex in water. In Ru(II)3, R1 is pyren-1-yl. R2 represents the substituent on the other tpy ligand. In Ru(II)1, R2 is a phenyl group, while in Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3, R2 is 4-BODIPY-phenyl. The BODIPY and pyrenyl substituents are predicted to impact the photophysical properties of the Ru(II) complex by extending the duration of triplet excited states. Three nanocomposites were developed, each incorporating a Ru(II) complex with different tpy ligand substitutions. These nanocomposites were named GQD‒Ru(II)1, GQD‒Ru(II)2, and GQD‒Ru(II)3. In all three structures, the GQD is linked to the Ru(II) complex via an amide bond. Li and colleagues utilized DFT calculations to thoroughly investigate various absorption modes related to amidation bonding, comparing it with radical addition and cycloaddition methods involving an organic molecule and the GQD [44]. Their detailed analysis conclusively demonstrated that amidation bonds offer better photoconversion efficiency than the other two methods. The various R1 and R2 substituents allowed for a systematic exploration of structure-function relationships and their influence on properties such as light absorption, excited-state dynamics, and photovoltaic performance in the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites. To further explore the energy conversion process, the tpy ligand was modified with a carboxyl group. This change was driven by the lowest excited state, facilitating electron injection from the dye to the semiconductor, characterized by a metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) transition [45]. Introducing the carboxyl group into the tpy ligand enhanced electron injection efficiency, leading to an overall improvement in energy conversion. To enhance dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), researchers have concentrated on the surface properties of titanium dioxide (TiO2), particularly the (101) surface of anatase, known for its low surface energy that boosts DSSC efficiency. Additionally, small TiO2 clusters, such as (TiO2)38, play a crucial role in facilitating charge transfer from dye sensitizers to TiO2. For instance, a study by Angelis et al. found that the absorption energy and band gap of the [Fe(CN)6]4−‒sensitized (TiO2)38 cluster system closely matched experimental results [46]. In this research, the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposite was attached to the surface of a (TiO2)38 cluster, as illustrated in Supplemental Figure S1.

The chemical reactivity of a nanocomposite is crucial for its ability to capture light effectively. This reactivity refers to how readily the nanocomposite undergoes chemical reactions, which directly impacts its performance in light harvesting. One key factor is the efficiency of light-induced electron transitions, which can be influenced by the nanocomposite's chemical hardness (η). Nanocomposites with lower hardness tend to facilitate more efficient electron transitions, thereby improving their light-harvesting capabilities. Another important factor is the chemical potential (μ) of the nanocomposite. A lower chemical potential increases the likelihood of successful light-induced charge separations, a critical process for efficient light harvesting. Additionally, the electron-donating power (ω−) of the nanocomposite also plays a significant role. This parameter measures the nanocomposite's ability to donate electrons. A lower ω− signifies a greater capacity to donate photogenerated electrons, which is essential for enhancing light harvesting. By understanding and optimizing these reactivity parameters—chemical hardness, chemical potential, and electron-donating power—it becomes possible to improve the light-harvesting efficiency of nanocomposites. Detailed methods for calculating these parameters can be found in the supplemental materials.

All calculations were performed using the BDF quantum chemistry program package [47–50]. Geometry optimizations of the ground state (S0) were conducted using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the B3LYP hybrid functional. B3LYP is widely used for its effective balance between computational efficiency and accuracy in predicting molecular geometries, electronic structures, and energetics, particularly in transition metal complexes such as ruthenium [51]. This makes it a preferred choice for large systems, like GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites. Studies on Ru(II) diimine complexes have shown that B3LYP reliably predicts the energy differences between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), as well as metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) properties, which align well with experimental data [52]. Studies of Ru(II)/polybipyridine complexes further highlight B3LYP's capability in addressing complex electronic interactions in these systems [53]. While functionals such as M06-2X and ωB97X-D are more accurate for modeling non-covalent interactions, they require more computational resources. Though these advanced functionals may offer slight improvements in certain cases, such as predicting ligand substitution energies, the differences are often not significant enough to justify the higher computational cost for large systems like GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites [54]. Therefore, while advanced functionals might be preferred when non-covalent interactions are essential and computational resources are available, B3LYP is an efficient and practical choice for most calculations involving large systems. Additionally, theoretical studies highlight the importance of including relativistic effects in electronic structure calculations for heavy elements like ruthenium. The optimization process follows specific convergence criteria to ensure a well-converged nanocomposite geometry. These criteria include thresholds for energy and gradients: the maximum force is set at 0.00045 Hartree/Bohr, and the root mean square (RMS) force at 0.0003 Hartree/Bohr, with the energy change less than 1 × 10−10 and the RMS density matrix element change under 5 × 10−8. The maximum displacement is limited to 0.0018 Bohr, and the RMS displacement to 0.0012 Bohr. Previous studies confirm that these thresholds are adequate for simulating GQD [55–57], metal-organic complexes [58,59] and GQD/metal-organic nanocomposites [24]. To ensure the optimized structures represent true energy minima rather than transition states, frequency calculations were conducted at the same theoretical level. The absence of frequencies with an imaginary component in the vibrational spectrum suggests that the optimized geometry corresponds to the lowest potential energy. If any imaginary frequencies were detected, the structure was re-optimized to achieve a true minimum. The scalar relativistic sf-X2C Hamiltonian was used with the x2c-SVPall basis set [60,61] for non-metal elements (H, B, C, N, O), while the x2c-TZVPall basis set [60–62] was applied to the ruthenium center. Relativistic effects are significant in heavy elements due to their high atomic numbers, and the x2c (exact two-component) basis sets are specifically designed to account for these effects [63–65]. The x2c-TZVPall basis set, with its triple-zeta quality, offers a precise description of ruthenium's electronic structure, which is crucial for understanding the properties and reactivity of ruthenium complexes [66,67]. The x2c-SVPall basis set, used for non-metal elements, balances computational efficiency with accurate valence orbital descriptions, making it suitable for studying large organic systems [68]. Using this mixed basis set approach allows researchers to achieve a compromise between computational cost and accuracy. This is more efficient than all-electron basis sets, which are computationally demanding, and more accurate than smaller basis sets like LANL2DZ, which may miss critical electronic details. The sf-X2C Hamiltonian is vital for accurately modeling transition metals by incorporating scalar relativistic effects, using a two-component Hamiltonian that corrects for both scalar-relativistic and spin-orbit effects [69]. The X2C transformation further ensures accurate conversion of nuclear hyperfine magnetic field operators and one-electron Hamiltonians, which is essential for predicting excited-state properties of transition metal complexes [70]. It has been demonstrated that this level of theory yields accurate excited state properties for transition metal complexes [48,71].

Using time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) [50,72] at the optimized ground state (S0) geometry, we calculated vertical excitation energies and oscillator strengths. The same functional (B3LYP), sf-X2C Hamiltonian, and basis sets that were employed in the ground state optimizations were used. Solvent effects were accounted for using the conductor-like screening model (COSMO) [73–75], with acetonitrile chosen as the solvent to align with experimental conditions [23]. Acetonitrile, a polar aprotic solvent, stabilizes charged and polar species common in metal complexes and their excited states [76,77]. It is chemically inert toward many metal complexes, minimizing side reactions [78,79], and has minimal absorption in the visible spectrum, which allows for clearer spectroscopic measurements [80]. Additionally, it provides good solubility for a range of organic and organometallic compounds [80–82]. COSMO, a continuum solvation model that approximates the solvent as a dielectric continuum around the solute, generally provides a good representation of solvation effects for both ground state properties and vertical excitations in acetonitrile [83–85]. This makes COSMO a reliable approximation for modeling solvent effects in the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites examined here. The calculations included the first 40 singlet excited states. To better understand the nature of the lowest excited state, an analysis was performed to provide insights into the charge transfer dynamics and interactions within the nanocomposite. This was achieved by quantifying the charge transfer distance (ΔL) and computing the interfragment charge transfer (IFCT) between the Ru(II) complex and the GQD. The analysis was carried out using the Multiwfn software [86], which calculates IFCT during electron excitation. It does so by first determining the contributions of individual atoms to both the hole (where an electron is missing after excitation) and the excited electron's location. These atomic contributions are then summed to determine the charge transfer contributions of entire molecular fragments, with the assumption that fragment contributions can be considered as the sum of their atomic parts. The ΔL is computed by applying a methodology designed to assess the character of charge transfer excitations and evaluate the accuracy of TD-DFT calculations. This approach involves calculating ΔL as the weighted average distance between the centroids of the orbitals involved in an excitation. The formula for ΔL is [87]:

Previous studies have successfully applied these methods to examine charge transfer interactions between GQD and catechin [88], as well as between GQD and an Ir(III) complex [89]. These methods provide valuable information on the electronic behavior of nanocomposite materials, aiding in the design and optimization of systems for energy conversion applications.

To further verify the reliability of the selected functional (B3LYP) and the mixed basis set (x2c-TZVPall/x2c-SVPall), additional calculations were performed, as shown in Figure S2. The basis set for Ru was varied from x2c-TZVPall to x2c-QZVPall, while keeping x2c-SVPall for the non-metals. This revealed a slight decrease in the bandgap when using the improved Ru basis set. Additionally, changing the non-metal basis set from x2c-SVall to x2c-SVPall-2c also led to a minor reduction in the bandgap. Testing various functionals with the same x2c-TZVPall/x2c-SVPall basis set demonstrated consistent bandgaps across different functionals. Overall, the bandgap remained relatively stable and showed little sensitivity to changes in the basis set, confirming the reliability of the B3LYP/x2c-TZVPall/x2c-SVPall approach. Furthermore, the absorption spectra of Ru(II)1, Ru(II)2, and Ru(II)3 were calculated using the B3LYP functional and x2c-TZVPall/x2c-SVPall mixed basis set (shown in Supplemental Figure S3). The first absorption band for Ru(II)2 was found to be sharper compared to those of Ru(II)1 and Ru(II)3, consistent with experimental results [23]. All complexes exhibited absorption bands in the 250–400 nm range, corresponding to 1π,π* transitions localized in the ligands (Supplemental Table S1). In contrast, the absorption bands in the 400–600 nm range were attributed to charge transfer transitions (Supplemental Table S2), such as ligand-to-metal charge transfer (1LMCT), metal-to-ligand charge transfer (1MLCT), intraligand charge transfer (1ILCT), and interligand charge transfer (1LLCT). These findings align well with previous theoretical studies, further supporting the accuracy of the computational approach [23].

Dye molecules must meet a crucial requirement: their LUMO energy level must be appropriately aligned to facilitate efficient electron transfer from the LUMO to the conduction band (CB) edge of the semiconductor oxide, typically titanium dioxide (TiO2). Additionally, the HOMO energy levels must be suitable for efficient electron retrieval from the electrolyte, often composed of iodide/triiodide ions (I−/I3−). Therefore, analyzing the distribution and energy levels of frontier molecular orbitals is essential for optimizing the performance of these dye-sensitized systems. The electron distributions in the HOMOs and LUMOs of isolated Ru(II) complexes and GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites are summarized in Table 1 , while the energy levels of the HOMOs (EHOMO) and LUMOs (ELUMO), along with the band gaps (Eg), are presented in Fig. 2 and Table 2 . For reference, the conduction band minimum (CBM; -4.0 eV [90,91]) of TiO2 and the redox potential (HOMO; -4.8 eV [90,91]) of I−/I3− are indicated by blue and red dashed lines, respectively. In Ru(II)1, the HOMO is predominantly centered on the Ru metal core, while the LUMO is primarily located on the tpy ligand. This suggests that light excitation likely involves charge transfer from the Ru center to the tpy ligand. In contrast, for Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3, the electron-donating BODIPY and pyrenyl groups shift the HOMO density towards these ligands instead of the metal core. Specifically, in Ru(II)2, the HOMO is concentrated on the 4-BODIPY-phenyl group, while in Ru(II)3, it is localized on the pyrenyl group. For all three Ru(II) complexes, the LUMOs are situated on the tpy ligands. Consequently, the ligand-based HOMOs in Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3 enable different excitation pathways involving charge transfers within the ligands. Upon attachment of GQDs, these nanomaterials seem to influence the HOMO and contribute additional LUMO density, enhancing charge separation (see Fig. 3 ). For instance, in GQD‒Ru(II)1, the GQD and Ru(II) complex share a delocalized LUMO, whereas in the other nanocomposites, the LUMOs remain localized within the Ru(II) complex. The delocalized LUMO of GQD‒Ru(II)1, compared to the localized LUMOs of the other nanocomposites, indicates that GQDs introduce virtual orbital density capable of interacting with the tpy ligand orbitals. The variations in substituents and the integration of GQDs result in consistent changes in the frontier orbitals, significantly impacting the properties relevant to solar energy conversion.

Generally, substituents with strong electron-donating properties increase the HOMO energy level of a metal-organic complex. Both BODIPY and pyrenyl substituents are examples of such electron-donating groups. They elevate the HOMO energy levels of the Ru(II) complexes through several key mechanisms. First, BODIPY and pyrenyl are large, planar aromatic systems that extend the π-conjugation of the terpyridine ligand. This delocalization of electrons over a larger area can destabilize occupied molecular orbitals by mixing filled metal d orbitals with filled ligand orbitals. This results in antibonding interactions that raise the energy of these orbitals [92], including the HOMO. As the electron density spreads out, the bonding interactions between the metal and ligand may weaken, leading to higher energy and less stable occupied orbitals. Additionally, these groups act as electron donors, pushing electron density towards the metal center and ligand framework, which destabilizes the highest occupied orbitals and raises their energy [93,94]. The steric bulk of these substituents may also slightly weaken metal-ligand bonding, reducing ligand field splitting and raising d orbital energies [95]. Most significantly, these substituents impose a ligand-centered HOMO. In Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3, the HOMO becomes localized on the BODIPY and pyrenyl substituents, respectively, rather than being metal-centered as in Ru(II)1. This localization introduces new high-energy occupied orbitals. Our results (see Table 1) reflect this, showing that the HOMO energies increase from -5.95 eV (Ru(II)1) to -5.68 eV (Ru(II)2) and -5.55 eV (Ru(II)3), with a shift in HOMO localization from the metal center to the substituents. The data in Table 1 indicates that among the isolated Ru(II) complexes, Ru(II)1 has the lowest HOMO and the highest LUMO, resulting in the largest band gap. This suggests that Ru(II)1 may exhibit lower chemical reactivity and reduced ability to absorb visible light compared to Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3. The weaker electron-donating and less conjugated substituents of Ru(II)1—namely the 4-{2-[2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy]ethoxy}phenyl and phenyl groups—contribute to its larger HOMO-LUMO gap, making it less reactive and less efficient in absorbing visible light. In contrast, the stronger electron-donating and highly conjugated BODIPY and pyrenyl groups in Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3 raise the HOMO energy and lower the LUMO energy, leading to smaller band gaps. This more favorable electronic structure enhances their reactivity and improves their ability to absorb lower-energy, visible light compared to Ru(II)1.

Incorporating GQD into the Ru(II) complexes leads to significant changes in their electronic structure. While the LUMO levels of the nanocomposites remain very similar to those of the corresponding isolated Ru(II) complexes, the HOMO is substantially destabilized in the nanocomposites compared to the isolated complexes. This indicates that GQD has little effect on the LUMO but strongly destabilizes the HOMO upon binding to the Ru(II) complexes. The minimal change in LUMO energy suggests that the GQD does not significantly interact with or perturb the ligand-based lowest unoccupied orbitals, indicating that a notable portion of the LUMO remains localized on the Ru(II) complex, thus preserving its electron-accepting character. The considerable destabilization of the HOMO implies that the GQD contributes significant new occupied orbital density at energies above the metal-based HOMO. Both the isolated Ru(II) complexes and the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites exhibit suitable HOMO and LUMO levels for ground-state regeneration and electron injection into TiO2. Specifically, the LUMO levels of Ru(II)1 and Ru(II)2, at -2.58 eV and -2.64 eV, respectively, provide appropriate energetic alignment with the TiO2 conduction band for efficient electron injection. In contrast, the lower LUMO of -2.66 eV for Ru(II)3 reduces the injection driving force and may hinder kinetics. Regarding ground-state dye regeneration, Ru(II)1 is thermodynamically most favorable due to its low-lying HOMO of -5.95 eV. The destabilized HOMO of -5.68 eV for Ru(II)2 decreases its regeneration potential, while Ru(II)3 has an intermediate HOMO level of -5.55 eV, offering a moderate driving force. The higher HOMO energy could slow down regeneration kinetics, while the preserved LUMO levels around -2.5 to -2.7 eV maintain favorable alignment for efficient electron injection into the TiO2 conduction band.

In Fig. 3, the projected density of states (PDOS) for isolated Ru(II) complexes and their nanocomposites is presented. The bandgap falls within the visible light region, enabling strong absorption and photoexcitation in the nanocomposites. When exposed to visible light, GQDs can generate charge-separated states, with electrons transitioning from the GQD's LUMO while holes remain in the GQD's HOMO. For all three nanocomposites, the transfer of photoexcited holes occurs within the Ru(II complex's HOMOs, while electron transfer is influenced by the relative positions of their LUMOs. Notably, in GQD‒Ru(II)2 and GQD‒Ru(II)3, photoelectrons generated in the GQD are transferred to the Ru(II) complex due to favorable energetic alignment. In contrast, for GQD‒Ru(II)1, the LUMO level is distributed between the GQD and the Ru(II) complex. Consequently, when initial photoexcitation takes place, there is an adiabatic charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, as indicated by the cyan arrow in Fig. 4 .

Fig. 5 illustrates the calculated absorption spectra of the three nanocomposites, each featuring different substituents. Notably, for GQD‒Ru(II)3, the first absorption peak (S1) shifts toward longer wavelengths compared to the other two nanocomposites. Additionally, the absorption maximum (Smax) for GQD‒Ru(II)3 moves toward shorter wavelengths. This behavior can be attributed to the pyrenyl substituent directly bonded to the terpyridine (tpy) ligand, which acts as an electron donor through its aromatic system. This interaction increases the energy separation between the GQD's π and π* orbitals, leading to a more stable configuration and a blueshift in the π-π* transition towards higher energies. In contrast, the BODIPY group in GQD‒Ru(II)2 does not have a direct connection with the tpy ligand, resulting in less effective electron donation from the GQD's π system. Consequently, the destabilization and blueshift of the tpy π-π* transition in GQD‒Ru(II)2 are less pronounced. The absorption spectrum of GQD‒Ru(II)2 resembles that of GQD‒Ru(II)1, particularly in the lower-energy region (500-650 nm). However, in the higher-energy region (400-500 nm), GQD‒Ru(II)2 exhibits greater absorbance than GQD‒Ru(II)1. For both GQD‒Ru(II)1 and GQD‒Ru(II)2, the transitions S1 and Smax originate from π-π* excitations on the GQDs (see supplemental Table S3). The GQD serves as a crucial light absorber, as indicated in the absorption spectrum. Upon photoexcitation, the electron distributions suggest a charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex occurs, regardless of whether it is in the S1 or Smax state. This charge transfer phenomenon is significant for enabling effective charge separation in the GQD‒Ru(II) systems. Specifically, for GQD‒Ru(II)3, the S1 transition is primarily attributed to local excitations based on the GQD and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) processes between Ru(II) and the tpy ligands. Conversely, the Smax state transition in GQD‒Ru(II)3 shows a significant GQD contribution, similar to that observed in GQD‒Ru(II)1 and GQD‒Ru(II)2.

The charge transfer length (ΔL), calculated by quantifying the spatial separation between electron and hole densities in excited states, offers important insights into the photoelectric performance of materials. It characterizes the efficiency of electron-hole separation following photoexcitation: a shorter ΔL indicates poor spatial separation between electrons and holes, leading to faster nonradiative recombination. This results in energy dissipation as heat rather than generating photocurrent. Conversely, a larger ΔL suggests well-separated charge densities, which hinders the relaxation pathway that leads to nonradiative recombination, thereby facilitating electron extraction for photocurrent generation. Thus, longer charge transfer lengths are advantageous for photoelectric applications, enabling more effective photoinduced charge separation. Table 3 presents the ΔL values for various complexes. Ru(II)1, featuring oligoether and phenyl substituents, has a ΔL of 2.98 Å. The addition of a BODIPY substituent in Ru(II)2 slightly increases ΔL to 3.11 Å. In contrast, the pyrenyl substituent in Ru(II)3 results in a significant increase in ΔL to 5.86 Å, indicating that this substituent enhances spatial charge separation most effectively. For the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, GQD‒Ru(II)1 has a ΔL of 5.05 Å, which is higher than that of isolated Ru(II)1. GQD‒Ru(II)2 exhibits a ΔL of 3.54 Å, comparable to isolated Ru(II)2. Notably, GQD‒Ru(II)3 shows a very large ΔL of 8.97 Å, significantly greater than that of isolated Ru(II)3. The presence of GQD boosts charge separation for Ru(II)1 and dramatically enhances it for Ru(II)3, demonstrating the effectiveness of GQD in improving charge transfer properties in these nanocomposites.

Interfragment charge transfer (ΔQ) is a crucial parameter for assessing the migration of electron density from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex within the nanocomposite. This process indicates the transfer of electron density to the dye following photon absorption, resulting in a hole left on the GQD. A decrease in ΔQ suggests that the excited electron is not effectively transferred from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, which can limit charge stabilization and lead to rapid recombination. In contrast, an increase in ΔQ reflects significant migration of the excited electron density towards the Ru(II) complex, promoting charge separation stabilization and reducing recombination rates. Therefore, enhancing interfragment charge transfer from the photoexcited GQD to the Ru(II) complex is beneficial for improving photoelectric efficiency by increasing charge stabilization on the Ru(II) complex and facilitating better retrieval of photoinduced electrons. Table 3 indicates that the pyrenyl substituent leads to the largest enhancement in interfragment charge transfer (ΔQ), increasing it to 0.75 e⁻. In comparison, ΔQ is 0.55 e⁻ for oligoether/phenyl and 0.65 e⁻ for BODIPY. This greater transfer for the pyrenyl substituent is likely attributed to several factors: its extended π-conjugation, which provides more delocalized electron density; the introduction of lower-lying π* orbitals; direct coordination with the Ru(II) complex that facilitates stronger electronic communication through d-π interactions; and potential steric effects that may weaken metal-ligand bonding. Additionally, the pyrenyl group imposes a ligand-centered HOMO, modifying the excitation pathway, in contrast to the other substituents that have less extensive conjugation, higher orbital energies, indirect linkages, and minimal steric effects. As a result, the pyrenyl substituent effectively enhances interfragment charge transfer and spatial separation of the photogenerated electron-hole pair more than the other substituents.

Parameters such as chemical hardness (η), chemical potential (μ), and electron-donating power (ω⁻) play a crucial role in influencing the material's reactivity and its effectiveness as a light sensitizer. Materials with lower η values generally produce a higher number of electron-hole pairs. A reduced η facilitates efficient electron transfer, while lower μ and ω⁻ values improve the material's ability to capture and donate electrons effectively.

The trend in η is Ru(II)1 > Ru(II)2 > Ru(II)3 (Table 1). Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3 have less negative μ values (-4.16 eV and -4.11 eV, respectively) compared to Ru(II)1 (-4.27 eV), indicating a lower tendency to accept electrons. Additionally, Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3 exhibit higher ω⁻ values (5.31 eV and 5.33 eV, respectively) than Ru(II)1 (5.25 eV), suggesting a reduced capacity to donate electrons. This diminished ability to accept and donate electrons in Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3 is attributed to the electronic effects of the BODIPY and pyrenyl substituents on the terpyridine ligands. The electron-rich BODIPY and pyrenyl groups increase electron density on the metal center and terpyridine ligand, reducing the complexes' ability to accept electrons and resulting in less negative μ values. Furthermore, the extended π-conjugation of these substituents stabilizes the HOMO of the complexes, making them less likely to donate electrons and leading to higher ω⁻ values. The steric bulk of the substituents may also weaken the metal-ligand bonding, further diminishing the electron-accepting and -donating capacities of Ru(II)2 and Ru(II)3. This reduced electron-donating ability may impact electron transfer dynamics in dye-sensitized solar cells, potentially slowing the regeneration of the oxidized dye by the redox couple (e.g., I⁻/I³⁻) after electron injection into the semiconductor, which could increase recombination losses as the oxidized dye may recapture the injected electron before regeneration.

Comparing GQD‒Ru(II)1 with GQD‒Ru(II)2, the addition of the BODIPY substituent has little effect on η, which starkly contrasts with the significant impact observed in Ru(II)1 and Ru(II)2, where the BODIPY substituent decreases η, indicating increased chemical reactivity. This discrepancy in chemical reactivity variation regarding BODIPY between GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites and isolated Ru(II) complexes stems from differing frontier orbital distributions. In GQD‒Ru(II)1, the LUMO is influenced by both the GQD and Ru(II)1, while in the isolated Ru(II) complex, it is dominated by the Ru(II) complex alone, leading to divergent reactivity patterns. The μ slightly decreases from -4.08 to -4.12 eV, enhancing electron-accepting ability. Meanwhile, ω⁻ increases from 5.61 to 5.67 eV with BODIPY, indicating modest improvements in electron-accepting character. For GQD‒Ru(II)3, the pyrenyl substituent reduces η to 2.43 eV, compared to 2.56-2.57 eV for the other nanocomposites, signifying increased chemical reactivity. It also lowers μ to -4.14 eV, improving electron-accepting capacity, while raising ω⁻ to 5.89 eV. Thus, pyrenyl enhances reactivity and electron-accepting capacity but reduces donating ability compared to other substituents. The lower η values for the nanocomposites compared to isolated Ru(II) complexes indicate that binding with GQD increases chemical reactivity, facilitating electron transfer and transitions beneficial for light harvesting and charge separation. The lower μ values in the nanocomposites suggest improved electron-accepting character, crucial for photocurrent generation. The ω⁻ values are similar between isolated complexes and nanocomposites, indicating that GQD binding does not significantly affect donating ability. Overall, the reduced η and μ values in the nanocomposites reflect enhanced chemical reactivity and electron-accepting behavior compared to isolated complexes, which is promising for photoelectric applications requiring efficient electron transfer and separation post-light absorption. The substituents modulate reactivity parameters less drastically than GQD binding, fine-tuning rather than significantly altering photoelectric properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free energy changes of electron injection and ground state regeneration
ce:section.ce:para: Free energy changes in electron injection (ΔGinj) and ground state regeneration (ΔGreg) are vital for efficient energy conversion in systems like DSSCs. Adequate free energy change for electron injection ensures a strong driving force, facilitating rapid electron transfer and minimizing recombination losses, thereby enhancing charge separation efficiency and maximizing electrical output. Similarly, favorable free energy changes for ground state regeneration enable the dye to efficiently revert to its ground state, reducing energy loss and maintaining the cycle. Balancing these changes optimizes energy conversion by ensuring rapid and efficient processes, minimizing recombination pathways, and preserving charges for productive work, which is essential for designing effective photovoltaic devices.

The ΔGinj values for isolated Ru(II) complexes range from -0.65 eV to -0.76 eV, indicating favorable thermodynamics for electron injection from the excited Ru(II) complex to the TiO2 conduction band. Ru(II)3, with a pyrenyl substituent, has the least negative ΔGinj at -0.65 eV, suggesting slightly less favorable electron injection than Ru(II)1 (-0.76 eV) and Ru(II)2 (-0.67 eV). Incorporating GQD significantly enhances the electron injection force, as shown by the more negative ΔGinj values. GQD‒Ru(II)1 displays the most negative ΔGinj at -1.74 eV, indicating the strongest thermodynamic driving force for electron injection among the three nanocomposites. In contrast, GQD‒Ru(II)2 and GQD‒Ru(II)3 have similar ΔGinj values of -1.21 eV and -1.19 eV, respectively. The increased driving force for electron injection upon GQD binding is attributed to the destabilization of the LUMO energy levels in the nanocomposites compared to isolated Ru(II) complexes. GQD introduces additional virtual orbital density that interacts with the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex, resulting in a lower LUMO energy level and a more negative ΔGinj value.

The ΔGreg values for isolated Ru(II) complexes are negative, ranging from -1.13 eV to -1.27 eV, indicating that the regeneration of the oxidized Ru(II) complex by the I⁻/I₃⁻ redox couple is thermodynamically favorable. Ru(II)1 has the strongest ground-state regeneration force, with a ΔGreg value of -1.27 eV. In contrast, binding with GQD results in less negative ΔGreg values, reflecting a reduction for ground-state regeneration in the nanocomposites compared to complexes alone. GQD‒Ru(II)1 has a ΔGreg of -0.18 eV, significantly less negative than isolated Ru(II)1 (-1.27 eV). GQD‒Ru(II)2 and GQD‒Ru(II)3 present similar ΔGreg values of -0.36 eV and -0.35 eV, respectively. This decrease in driving force for ground-state regeneration in the nanocomposites is attributed to the destabilization of the HOMO energy levels upon GQD binding, as illustrated in Fig. 2. The GQD introduces significant new occupied orbital density above the metal-based HOMO, leading to lower HOMO energy levels and, consequently, less negative ΔGreg values. The impact of substituents on ΔGreg in isolated Ru(II) complexes is relatively minor, with Ru(II)1 showing the most negative value (-1.27 eV), followed by Ru(II)2 (-1.14 eV) and Ru(II)3 (-1.13 eV). However, in the nanocomposites, substituents have a negligible effect on ΔGreg, as seen with GQD‒Ru(II)2 (-0.36 eV) and GQD‒Ru(II)3 (-0.35 eV).

The photoelectric energy conversion in GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites is crucial due to their capacity for broad sunlight absorption across the solar spectrum, resulting from the complementary absorption profiles of GQDs and Ru(II) complexes with tailored ligands. These nanocomposites facilitate efficient charge separation through cascaded energy level alignment, allowing photoexcited electrons in the GQD to inject into the LUMO of the Ru(II) complex. This process extends the lifetime of the charge-separated state and enhances charge transport for electricity generation. Furthermore, the optoelectronic properties of the nanocomposites can be tuned by modifying the substituents on the Ru(II) complex ligands, optimizing light absorption, charge separation, and energy level alignment with semiconductor substrates, ultimately improving overall photoelectric conversion efficiency.

The energy conversion efficiency (Ω) is obtained by:
(4)

where Ω represents the percentage of incident solar power successfully converted into electricity; Voc (open circuit voltage) is the maximum voltage across the solar cell when no current flows. FF (Fill Factor) reflects the "squareness" of the solar cell's current-voltage curve. It represents the ratio of the maximum power output of the cell to the product of Voc and Jsc and can be obtained according to reference paper [91]. A higher fill factor indicates a more ideal solar cell with lower resistive losses; Pin (Incident Power) is the total power of the incident sunlight illuminating the solar cell, typically measured in watts per square meter (W/m²); Jsc (short-circuit current) is the maximum current that flows through the solar cell when the voltage across it is zero (short-circuited).

The Voc is determined by the difference in energy levels between the electron donor and acceptor materials [91,97–99]:

The Jsc depends on the amount of light absorbed by the dye and the efficiency of charge separation and collection. The Jsc is given by [91,97–99]:

Coulombs), representing the charge of a single electron; LHE(λ) signifies the light harvesting efficiency at a specific wavelength (λ). It quantifies how effectively the dye molecule absorbs photons of a particular wavelength. LHE(λ) can be calculated using the oscillator strength (σ) at the specified wavelength by
LHE

, where σ(λ) is oscillator strength at the specific wavelength; ϕinj represents the quantum yield of electron injection, indicating the probability of a photoexcited electron in the dye successfully transferring to the conduction band of TiO2. This parameter depends on factors such as the energy level alignment between the dye's LUMO and the TiO2 conduction band edge, as well as the electronic coupling between the two materials; φcollect signifies the charge collection efficiency, representing the fraction of injected electrons that are successfully collected at the electrode and contribute to the measured current. Charge collection efficiency is influenced by factors such as electron transport properties within the TiO2 and the presence of recombination losses; Is(λ) represents the incident photon flux density at a specific wavelength (λ), essentially quantifying the number of photons of that wavelength striking the solar cell per unit area and time. This value depends on the solar spectrum and the specific illumination conditions.

where tinj denotes the time constant for electron injection, representing the characteristic time scale for the electron transfer process from the dye's excited state to the TiO2 conduction band. A shorter tinj indicates a faster injection process; trelax signifies the time constant for relaxation processes, encompassing all other pathways by which the excited dye molecule can lose its energy and return to the ground state without injecting an electron into TiO2, and is typically 10 ps [100,101].

The φcollect is given by:
(8)
φ
c o l l e c t
=
1

t
t r a n s
t
r e c
where ttrans denotes the time constant for electron transport, representing the characteristic time scale for the injected electron to travel through the TiO2 film and reach the collecting electrode, and is typically 5 ps [100]. A shorter ttrans indicates faster electron transport and a higher probability of successful collection; trec signifies the time constant for recombination processes, encompassing all pathways by which the injected electron can be lost before reaching the electrode. These recombination pathways can include back-transfer of the electron to the oxidized dye molecule or to the electrolyte. A shorter trec indicates faster recombination and a lower probability of successful charge collection.

Among the three nanocomposites, GQD‒Ru(II)3 features the longest charge transfer distance at 8.97 Å and the highest charge movement from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, totaling 0.75 e⁻. The addition of the pyrenyl substituent significantly enhances charge separation in GQD‒Ru(II)3, reducing the likelihood of recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes with TiO2, thereby shortening trec. Several factors related to its electronic structure contribute to this enhanced charge separation. The large, planar pyrenyl group extends the π-conjugation of the terpyridine ligand, decreasing overlap between electron and hole densities, which slows charge recombination. Additionally, the pyrenyl substituent introduces low-energy π* orbitals that act as electron acceptors, providing pathways for photoexcited electrons to migrate away from the GQD and stabilize the charge-separated state. The direct bonding of the pyrenyl group to the terpyridine ligand improves electronic communication with the Ru(II) complex via d-π interactions, enhancing charge transfer efficiency from the photoexcited GQD. The bulky pyrenyl group may also introduce steric repulsion, slightly destabilizing the metal-ligand bond and lowering barriers for electron transfer, thus promoting better charge separation. Furthermore, the pyrenyl group introduces a ligand-centered HOMO in Ru(II)3, which modifies the excitation pathway compared to the metal-centered HOMO in Ru(II)1, favoring charge separation by localizing the hole on the pyrenyl group and distancing it from the electron density in the GQD. In contrast, GQD‒Ru(II)2 has the shortest charge transfer distance at 3.54 Å, and its BODIPY substituent provides little improvement in charge separation compared to GQD‒Ru(II)1. The close proximity of electrons and holes in GQD‒Ru(II)2 may lead to faster recombination, resulting in a longer trec. GQD‒Ru(II)1 falls in between, with a moderate charge transfer distance of 5.05 Å and an interfragment charge transfer of 0.55 e⁻. It also has the most negative ΔGinj at -1.74 eV, indicating a strong drive for electron injection, which could result in faster charge separation and a longer trec compared to GQD‒Ru(II)2 and GQD‒Ru(II)3. The ΔGreg values are similar for all three, though slightly less negative for GQD‒Ru(II)1. A more negative ΔGreg implies a better chance for dye regeneration, helping prevent recombination. The less negative ΔGreg for GQD‒Ru(II)1 suggests a slightly slower regeneration process, potentially leading to shorter trec compared to the other two. Overall, considering charge separation and energy changes, the calculated trec values follow this order: GQD‒Ru(II)3 > GQD‒Ru(II)1 > GQD‒Ru(II)2.

A longer trec means the injected electrons stick around longer, keeping a bigger voltage gap between the dye and TiO2, resulting in a higher Voc. Conversely, a shorter tinj means electrons get injected faster, potentially boosting φinj and leading to a higher Voc. For the three GQD‒Ru(II) nanocomposites (Table 4 ), tinj happens much faster than trelax, so φinj can be considered nearly instantaneous. This suggests that injection speed is not the main factor affecting Voc in these systems. Instead, it is the trec that varies among them, with GQD‒Ru(II)3 having the longest trec, followed by GQD‒Ru(II)1 and GQD‒Ru(II)2. This difference in trec is likely what matters most for Voc, as a longer trec can maintain the voltage gap between the dye and TiO2 better, resulting in a higher Voc. Moreover, the calculated trec for the three GQD‒Ru(II) nanocomposites are on the nanosecond timescale, which aligns well with the typical electron-hole recombination times observed between the CBM of TiO2 and the HOMO of the Ru(II) complex [102,103].

Based on the calculated data of trec and tinj, we are able to estimate the ϕinj and φcollect, respectively. Since the trec of three nanocomposites are orders of magnitudes longer than the trans and the tinj of three nanocomposites are orders of magnitudes shorter than the trelax; therefore, the ϕinj and φcollect can be treated as unity. As such, the Jsc can be rewritten as:
(9)

Based on Eq. (9), the short-circuit current is mainly affected by light harvesting efficiency.

To determine the FF, it is necessary to first derive the current-voltage (J-V) relationship of the solar cell, as described by Eq. (10) [35]:

where kb is the Boltzmann constant; T is the temperature in Kelvin; q is the elementary charge (the charge of a single electron); n is the diode quality factor, or the ideality factor; generally it is between 1 and 2 where 1 denotes perfection with theoretical limit values. The presence of multiple recombination types often makes precise predictions of the n value impossible. However, using a value between 1 and 2 can yield more reliable results [35]. This equation governs the behavior of the J-V curve, which is a fundamental characteristic of solar cells and is essential for assessing photovoltaic performance. The J-V curve can be plotted using this relationship to visually represent the solar cell's operational behavior.

The saturation current density Js, required for the calculation of the J-V curve, is obtained from Eq. (11) [35]:

Once the saturation current density Js is calculated, it becomes possible to plot the J-V curve (supplemental Figure S4) using Eq. (10). From the J-V curve, the maximum power point (MPP) can be identified, where the product of voltage and current is maximized. With the maximum power point (MPP) identified as (Vmp,Jmp), the FF can be computed using the following expression:

where Vmp and Jmp are the voltage and current density at the maximum power point.

The GQD‒Ru(II)3 nanocomposite exhibits the highest energy conversion efficiency of 19.3 %, which can be attributed to the efficient light harvesting efficiency, as indicated by the highest short-circuit current density (Jsc = 16.3 mA/cm2. The GQD‒Ru(II)2 nanocomposite has the second-highest energy conversion efficiency of 18.2 %, while the GQD‒Ru(II)1 nanocomposite has the lowest energy conversion efficiency of 14.6 % due to lower short-circuit current density (Jsc = 11.2 mA/cm2) than the other two nanocomposites, indicating less efficient light harvesting.

The changes in excited states lead to photogenerated charge transfer, which drives the observed photoreactivity through harmonic generation. Total hyperpolarizability (κtot) is significant as it indicates a molecule's ability to facilitate charge separation by altering its electronic structure. High hyperpolarizability implies greater polarizability and a uniform charge distribution, which, as mentioned earlier, enhances electron injection into semiconductors.

The κtot values for the isolated Ru(II) complexes are relatively small (Table 5 ), ranging from 8.47 × 10−5 to 9.65 × 10−5 e.s.u. These low values suggest the isolated Ru(II) complexes have limited ability to undergo charge separation, which is crucial for enhancing photoinduced electron injection into semiconductor materials. The κtot values for the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites are significantly higher compared to the isolated Ru(II) complexes. GQD‒Ru(II)1 has a κtot of 0.020 e.s.u., GQD‒Ru(II)2 has 0.035 e.s.u., and GQD‒Ru(II)3 has the highest value of 0.085 e.s.u. The substantial increase in κtot upon incorporating the GQD into the Ru(II) complexes indicates that the nanocomposite structures exhibit enhanced ability to undergo charge separation. The increased hyperpolarizability in the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites compared to the isolated Ru(II) complexes can be attributed to several factors. First, the formation of the nanocomposite allows for more efficient charge transfer between the GQD and the Ru(II) complex, which facilitates the separation of charges. Second, the substituents, such as BODIPY and pyrenyl, provide extended π-conjugation in the nanocomposite structures. This can enhance the delocalization of electrons, leading to a higher propensity for charge separation. Third, the combination of the GQD and the Ru(II) complex with tailored ligands creates synergistic effects that amplify the nonlinear optical response, beyond what could be achieved with the individual components. The significantly enhanced total hyperpolarizability of the GQD‒Ru(II) nanocomposites, especially GQD‒Ru(II)3 with the pyrenyl substituent, suggests that these materials could exhibit improved photoinduced electron injection efficiency into semiconductor substrates, such as TiO2. This can lead to enhanced photovoltaic performance by facilitating more effective charge separation and transport, ultimately improving the overall energy conversion efficiency of the solar cell devices.

This study provides a comprehensive computational investigation into the optimization of photovoltaic performance in GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites through strategic ligand modifications. The substituents attached to the terpyridine ligand of the Ru(II) complex were found to have a profound impact on the electronic structure, charge transfer dynamics, and overall energy conversion efficiency of the nanocomposites.

The pyrenyl substituent emerged as the most effective in enhancing spatial charge separation and interfragment charge transfer from the GQD to the Ru(II) complex, leading to the highest energy conversion efficiency of 19.3 % among the studied nanocomposites. In contrast, the oligoether/phenyl and BODIPY substituents exhibited lower efficiencies of 14.6 % and 18.2 %, respectively. The significantly increased total hyperpolarizability of the GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites, particularly with the pyrenyl substituent, suggests improved photoinduced electron injection into semiconductor substrates, which can further optimize the photovoltaic performance.

These findings highlight the crucial role of strategic ligand modifications in tuning the optoelectronic properties and charge dynamics within GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites for efficient solar energy conversion applications. By leveraging computational methods, this study provides valuable insights that can guide the rational design of next-generation photovoltaic materials and devices. However, it is important to note that the calculated energy conversion efficiencies, while promising, are lower than some other reported sensitizers in the literature. This limitation underscores the need for further improvements through strategies such as heteroatom doping and surface functionalization of the GQDs, which can enhance light absorption and charge separation. Overall, the presented approach demonstrates the potential of GQD/Ru(II) nanocomposites as viable candidates for sustainable and high-performance photovoltaic technologies.
